sections.about: This metric measures R&D expenditure in specific technologies to improve environmental and social impacts as a percentage of total R&D expenditure. It falls under Principle 2, Essential Indicator 1 of the BRSR format. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata and JSW are tied at 100% across all three years, while SAIL remains around 9–11% and Jindal rises sharply from 0.02% in FY2023 to 34.8% in FY2024 and 51.98% in FY2025. Tata’s 100% reflects its disclosure approach: FY2025 BRSR states that R&D cost includes expenditure on environmental and sustainability initiatives such as low-emission transition, freshwater reduction, waste value maximisation, energy efficiency, circular economy, and use of low-grade raw materials. SAIL explicitly notes that although 100% of R&D may in some way improve environment or society, its reported 10.48% reflects only direct and specific R&D in relevant technologies. Jindal’s FY2025 R&D includes clad plates, stainless rebars, 415 grade stainless steel, corrosion investigation, and 20Cr5Al grade development for product longevity, localisation, import substitution, and lifecycle impact reduction.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks joint 1st at 100%, tied with JSW. Jindal is closest at 51.98%, which is 48.02 percentage points behind Tata and JSW. Tata’s ranking held steady at rank 1 across all three years, but the figure should be framed carefully: it reflects Tata’s broad classification of R&D alignment with NGRBC principles, not necessarily that every rupee is a discrete decarbonisation R&D project. The real strength is Tata’s ability to connect R&D to BF-BOF efficiency, low-grade raw material use, circularity, waste valorisation, water stewardship, and low-emission transition.
sections.targets: No R&D-percentage target identified for this metric. Tata links the disclosure to its ESG goals and factsheet; Jindal’s net-zero 2050 and 50% emission-intensity reduction before FY35 provide the strategic target context for sustainability-oriented R&D, but not a specific R&D allocation target.
sections.comparabilityNotes: This metric has a major methodology issue: Tata reports 100% because all R&D is aligned with one or more NGRBC principles, while SAIL reports only direct and specific R&D expenditure for environmental/social technologies. Therefore, rank is directionally useful but not fully like-for-like.

flags.flagNote: Methodology differences materially affect comparability, especially Tata/JSW at 100% versus SAIL’s direct-specific R&D basis.
